content.text: Customer-Reported AI Issue Intake Flow

The intake flow is the path a customer-reported issue takes from initial submission to the point where it enters the bug tracking system or is resolved without escalation. A well-designed intake flow routes issues to the right queue quickly, ensures evidence is collected before escalation, and gives customers clear acknowledgment without requiring engineering team involvement.

GenAI applications have a more complex intake flow than traditional software because the issue type is harder to determine from the initial report. 'The model gave a wrong answer' could be a model quality bug, a prompt engineering issue, an expected model limitation, or a safety event. The intake flow must classify which before routing.

attrs.alt: Customer-reported AI issue intake flow: 5-branch classification from L1

content.text: The Five Issue Types at Intake
At the point of first contact (L1), every customer-reported issue is classified into one of five categories. The classification determines the immediate routing:
Category
Definition
L1 action
Routed to
Safety concern
Customer reports harmful content, policy violation, safety filter issue, or PII exposure
Log verbatim; activate safety fast path immediately
Safety team + L3 (within 30 min)
API / infrastructure error
HTTP error codes, timeouts, authentication failures, rate limit issues
Collect request ID, error code, timestamp; check status page
L2 technical queue
Quality complaint
Model output wrong, unhelpful, or unexpected quality
Collect verbatim input/output; do not diagnose; escalate to L2
L2 with evidence packet
KB resolvable
Issue matches an existing runbook, known limitation, or documented workaround
Apply runbook; resolve at L1; log outcome
Closed at L1
Account / billing
Subscription, quota, key management, billing queries
Handle with account tools; escalate to billing team if needed
L1 + billing team

The classification is made by L1 in the first 5 minutes of receiving the ticket. If the issue category is unclear, L1 defaults to collecting evidence and escalating — the cost of an unnecessary escalation is low; the cost of misrouting a safety concern is high.
Safety Issue Intake: The Fast Path
Safety concerns bypass the normal classification flow. The moment L1 identifies a safety-related report, the fast path activates:
Safety Keywords That Activate the Fast Path
Train L1 agents to recognize these patterns in customer reports:
Reports of instructions to cause harm to self or others
Reports of content that appears to be from another user's session (possible data leakage)
Reports of content involving minors in inappropriate contexts
Reports of discriminatory, hateful, or targeted harassment content generated by the model
Reports of the model refusing to apply safety filters when specifically asked about them
Reports of model providing dangerous technical instructions (synthesis routes, weapon modifications)
Reports of safety filter blocking clearly legitimate professional content (medical, legal, security research)

The last category (safety filter over-refusal) is a safety issue, not just a quality complaint. Over-refusal can push users toward workarounds that undermine the filter entirely. Route it through the safety fast path so the Safety team is aware of the pattern, even if the immediate impact is lower severity.
Fast Path Steps for L1
Copy the customer's report verbatim into the ticket — do not paraphrase or summarize
Tag the ticket as  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'safety-concern', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  in the ticketing system
Post immediately to the safety escalation Slack channel (e.g.,  {'type': 'text', 'text': '#safety-escalations', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]} ) with the ticket ID and a one-line summary
Send the customer an acknowledgement: 'Thank you for reporting this. We are treating this as a priority and will investigate immediately.' — do not include any assessment of the report's validity
Do  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'not', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  ask the customer for more details before escalating — Safety team will follow up if needed
L2 picks up the safety ticket within 30 minutes and either confirms the escalation to L3 + Safety team or reclassifies if the report was not actually safety-related
Quality Complaint Intake: Evidence Collection

Quality complaints are the highest-volume non-trivial category for GenAI support. The intake flow for quality complaints has two goals: collect enough evidence that L2 can investigate without going back to the customer, and give the customer an informed acknowledgement that sets reasonable expectations.
Evidence Collection Checklist at L1

QUALITY COMPLAINT INTAKE CHECKLIST (L1)
========================================

Step 1 — Confirm it is a quality complaint (not an API error)
  Question: "Did you receive an error code or message, or did the model return
  a response that seemed incorrect or unhelpful?"
  If error code: route to infrastructure intake flow instead.

Step 2 — Request verbatim evidence
  "To investigate this effectively, I need the exact text you sent to the model
  and the exact response you received. Could you share both as copy-paste text
  or a screenshot?"
  [If customer cannot share exact text: ask them to reproduce and send]

Step 3 — Collect technical context
  - Which model version or API endpoint? (check account dashboard if unknown)
  - Approximate time of the request (UTC preferred)
  - Request ID (from API response headers — guide customer to find it if needed)

Step 4 — Assess business impact
  "How is this affecting your use case? Is this blocking you from completing
  a task, or is it degrading the quality of an output you can still use?"
  [Record: blocking / degrading / informational]

Step 5 — Check KB
  Search KB with key terms from the customer's complaint.
  If match found: apply runbook, resolve at L1, record KB hit.
  If no match: proceed to escalation.

Step 6 — Acknowledge and set expectations
  "We've received your report and will have a technical engineer review this.
  Given your [account tier], you can expect an initial response within [SLA].
  Your ticket reference is [ID]."

Step 7 — Escalate to L2 with full evidence packet attached
What Not to Tell the Customer at Intake
L1 must not make assessments or commitments that require technical knowledge:
Do not say
Why
Say instead
"That sounds like a bug in the model"
L1 cannot determine this; may be a prompt issue
"We're investigating the behavior you described"
"This will be fixed in the next update"
L1 has no visibility into the fix timeline
"Our engineering team will review and provide an update within [SLA]"
"Try rephrasing your prompt"
May be valid advice but inappropriate before L2 confirms it's a prompt issue
"Let me get this to our technical team first"
"The model sometimes makes mistakes"
Dismissive; may be a real regression
"We take quality reports seriously and will investigate"
"I think the safety filter triggered"
L1 should not guess; safety filter activation needs confirmation
"Let me escalate this for technical review"
Infrastructure Error Intake
API and infrastructure errors are the most structured intake path because they have a defined set of error codes with known causes. L1 can resolve many of these from the runbook without escalation.
Error Code Triage at Intake
HTTP status
Common cause
L1 action
Escalate?
400 Bad Request
Malformed request body, invalid parameter
Guide customer to correct parameter format; link to API docs
Only if customer insists correct format is being used
401 Unauthorized
Invalid or expired API key
Guide customer to regenerate key in dashboard
No — L1 resolves
429 Too Many Requests
Rate limit exceeded
Show customer their quota usage; explain limits; offer upgrade path
No — L1 resolves
500 Internal Server Error
Platform-side error
Check status page; if no incident, collect request ID + timestamp for L2
Yes — L2 investigates
503 Service Unavailable
Platform overloaded or maintenance
Check status page; inform customer of known incident or collect evidence
Yes if no active incident
408 / timeout
Request timeout
Collect request details; check if large context window or high concurrency
Yes if customer cannot workaround
When to Check the Status Page First

Before collecting evidence for any infrastructure error, L1 should check the platform status page. If an active incident explains the error, L1 informs the customer and links to the status page — no escalation needed. If no incident is listed but errors are occurring, L1 collects evidence and escalates to L2, who may initiate incident declaration if the errors are widespread.
Customer Communication Standards at Intake

The acknowledgement a customer receives at intake sets their expectations for the rest of the support interaction. For GenAI issues, where resolution timelines vary widely (minutes for configuration issues, weeks for model quality improvements), the acknowledgement must be calibrated to the issue type.
Acknowledgement Templates by Issue Category

TEMPLATE A: Safety concern (immediate)
---------------------------------------
Subject: Your safety report [Ticket #XXXXXX]

Thank you for reporting this. We treat reports of this nature as our highest
priority. A dedicated team is reviewing your report immediately.

You do not need to take any action. We will contact you with an update within
[1 business hour for enterprise / 4 hours for self-serve].

Ticket reference: [ID]

---

TEMPLATE B: Quality complaint (standard)
-----------------------------------------
Subject: We received your quality report [Ticket #XXXXXX]

Thank you for sharing the details of what you experienced. We've escalated
your report to our technical team for investigation.

Our team will review the interaction you described and respond with their
findings within [4 hours for enterprise / 24 hours for self-serve].

Ticket reference: [ID]

---

TEMPLATE C: Infrastructure error (status page active)
------------------------------------------------------
Subject: Service issue update [Ticket #XXXXXX]

We are aware of an issue affecting [service component] and are actively
investigating. You can follow real-time updates at: [status page URL]

We expect to have more information available by [ETA from status page].
Your ticket [ID] will be updated when the issue is resolved.

---

TEMPLATE D: KB resolved (L1 resolution)
-----------------------------------------
Subject: Update on your support request [Ticket #XXXXXX]

Thank you for reaching out. Based on the details you shared, [brief
explanation of what caused the issue and what the solution is].

[Specific steps or workaround]

Please let us know if this resolves the issue, or if you continue to
experience problems and we'll investigate further.

Ticket reference: [ID]
Intake Flow Metrics
Track these metrics to assess intake flow health:
Metric
Target
What it indicates
Time from ticket creation to L1 classification
< 30 min
L1 staffing and ticket queue depth
Safety fast path activation rate
Monitor for trends
Rate should be low and stable; spikes indicate safety events or new adversarial patterns
Evidence collection completeness at L1
> 85% of escalated tickets have full evidence
L1 training and intake form effectiveness
KB deflection rate at intake
> 40% of quality complaints
KB coverage of known issues
Customer acknowledgement sent within SLA
L1 process adherence
Reclassification rate by L2
< 10% of L1 classifications changed
L1 classification training quality

The reclassification rate is particularly informative: if L2 regularly reclassifies L1's category assignments, the classification criteria are ambiguous or L1 training is insufficient. Review reclassified tickets monthly to find patterns.
Handling Edge Cases at Intake
Customer Cannot Provide Exact Input/Output
Some customers report issues without retaining the exact input or output (they closed the window, refreshed the page, did not copy the text). L1 should:
Ask if the customer can reproduce the issue and share the exact text from a new attempt
If reproduction is not possible: accept the description and flag the ticket as 'evidence incomplete — cannot reproduce'
For S2-level issues (enterprise customer, blocking use case): L2 attempts to reproduce independently with similar inputs
For S3/S4 issues: log and monitor for additional reports from other customers with the same description — pattern matching substitutes for reproduction evidence
Customer Reports Multiple Issues in One Ticket
Customers frequently bundle multiple issues into one support ticket. L1 should:
Identify the most severe issue in the ticket — this determines the SLA and routing
Split the ticket if the issues require different routing (e.g., one safety concern + one billing question)
Inform the customer: 'I've split your request into two tickets: [ID-A] for [issue A] and [ID-B] for [issue B]. You'll receive updates on each separately.'
Repeat Reporter Patterns
Some customers report the same class of issue repeatedly. This can indicate:
A real bug that has not been fixed and keeps affecting them
A misunderstanding of the product that was not resolved by prior L2 communication
An adversarial user probing the system systematically

Flag repeat reporters at intake (most ticketing systems support this automatically). L2 should review prior ticket history before investigating — the prior investigation context avoids starting from scratch.
Routing to the Bug Tracker
Not every customer-reported issue becomes a bug ticket. Routing to the bug tracker is a deliberate decision made by L2 after investigation, not automatically at intake. The rules:
L2 investigation outcome
Bug tracker action
Confirmed reproduction of a model quality issue not previously known
File new bug: S/P assigned by L3 at daily triage
Matches an existing open bug
Link customer ticket to existing bug; update customer with bug ID and status
Confirmed infrastructure error not previously known
File new bug AND consider incident declaration if pattern is emerging
Customer education issue (prompt engineering, parameter misuse)
Close ticket as 'resolved — customer education'; no bug filed
Expected model behavior (documented limitation)
Close ticket as 'known limitation'; link KB article; no bug filed
Safety concern confirmed
File bug AND safety incident simultaneously; bug tagged safety-critical
Cannot reproduce, evidence insufficient
Close as 'cannot reproduce'; request customer to reopen with more evidence

The 'customer education' and 'known limitation' closures are the most important to get right. Under-classifying real bugs as 'expected behavior' means real quality issues accumulate without engineering attention. Over-classifying prompt issues as bugs means the bug tracker fills with noise that distracts engineering from real problems.
Bug Ticket Quality from Customer Reports

A bug ticket created from a customer report should be a standalone investigation brief, not a summary of the customer's complaint. The L2 engineer who creates the ticket writes it for the L3 engineer who will investigate, not for the customer:

BAD bug ticket (created directly from customer report):
-------------------------------------------------------
Title: Customer says model is giving wrong answers
Description: Acme Corp reported that the model is not working correctly
for their use case. They are unhappy with the output quality.
Assigned to: L3 team

---

GOOD bug ticket (created by L2 after investigation):
------------------------------------------------------
Title: [claude-3-sonnet] Legal document summarization omits key clauses
       when context > 80k tokens

Description:
Reported by: Acme Corp (Enterprise) — TKT-88234

Reproduction:
  Input: Legal contract (attached), summarisation prompt (attached)
  Context size: ~87k tokens
  Output: Summary omits indemnification clause (present in source, absent in summary)
  Reproduction rate: 4/5 attempts with this contract; 0/5 with shortened contract

Layer isolation:
  Retrieval: N/A (full document in context, no RAG)
  Context construction: Checked — indemnification clause is present in the passed context
  Generation: Clause is in the model's context window but not appearing in summary
              → generation-layer issue

Hypothesis:
  Context window truncation effect or attention falloff on later-document content
  at high token counts. Similar to known issue #441 but with different trigger point.

Related: Bug #441 (closed, different token threshold)

Severity: S2 (core use case for enterprise customer; workaround: shorten document)
Priority: P2 (workaround exists; target next sprint)
Owner: ML platform team
Cross-Channel Issue Intake

Customer-reported issues do not arrive only through the official support ticket system. GenAI platforms receive issue reports through multiple channels, each with different characteristics and requiring different intake handling:
Non-Ticket Intake Channels
Channel
Issue characteristics
Intake action
Support email
Often informal; may lack technical context; good starting point for evidence collection
L1 converts to ticket; sends acknowledgement; requests structured evidence
Community forum / Discord
Public; may contain reproductions; often discovered by the company before customers formally report
Monitor for quality or safety signals; create internal ticket if issue confirmed; respond publicly only after L2 confirms nature of issue
GitHub issues (public SDK repo)
Developer-facing; often high-quality technical reports with code; public visibility
L3 triages directly; link to internal bug tracker; respond on GitHub after triage
Social media (Twitter/X)
Often lacks technical context; high visibility; may go viral
Account manager or support lead responds publicly with generic acknowledgement; internal ticket created immediately
Enterprise account manager
High context; often represents premium customer complaints before formal ticket
AM creates ticket on customer's behalf; tagged as enterprise-escalation

The key rule for non-ticket channels:  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'always create a formal ticket', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} , even if the issue appears minor. Cross-channel issues that are not formalized get lost. The ticket is the single source of truth; social media DMs and Slack messages are not.
Proactive Intake: Monitoring-Triggered Issues

Some issues enter the intake flow from monitoring, not from customers. When observability alerts trigger before customer reports arrive, the intake flow runs in reverse: engineering creates the ticket and notifies affected customers proactively, rather than waiting for customers to report.
The proactive intake flow:
Observability alert fires (e.g., safety filter trigger rate spike, P99 latency above threshold)
L2 or L3 on-call investigates the alert and determines customer impact
If customer impact is confirmed: L2 creates an internal bug ticket before any customer reports arrive
Account managers are notified for enterprise customers who may be affected
Status page is updated if the impact is ongoing
When customers do report the same issue: link their ticket to the existing internal ticket — they are not starting a new investigation, they are adding data points to one already underway
Proactive intake is the hallmark of a mature GenAI support operation. It demonstrates to customers that the company monitors quality and safety actively, not just reactively.
Summary

The customer-reported AI issue intake flow classifies incoming reports into five categories at L1: safety concerns (safety fast path, within 30 minutes), API/infrastructure errors (runbook or L2), quality complaints (evidence collection then L2), KB-resolvable issues (resolved at L1), and account/billing (L1 + billing team). Safety concerns bypass normal routing and trigger the fast path immediately — no waiting for the next triage meeting. Quality complaint intake requires verbatim evidence collection before escalation; L1 does not attempt to diagnose model behavior. Customer acknowledgements are templated by issue category to set accurate expectations. Intake flow health is measured through classification latency, evidence completeness, KB deflection rate, and the L2 reclassification rate.
Key takeaways:
Five intake categories: safety (fast path), infrastructure (runbook/L2), quality (evidence + L2), KB match (L1 resolve), account/billing (L1)
Safety fast path activates immediately on keyword recognition — no waiting for classification review
Quality complaints require verbatim input/output before escalation — L1 does not paraphrase
L1 does not make technical assessments to customers at intake — no 'that sounds like a bug' or 'try rephrasing'
Reclassification rate by L2 (target < 10%) measures L1 classification training quality
Repeat reporters are flagged automatically; L2 reviews prior history before re-investigating
